content.full_text:
THE POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
50 K Street
Vallejo 94511
(707) 322-5660

October 20, 1975

The Hon. Dennis E. Carpenter
Senator, 36th District
State Capitol
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Request for Opinion
    No. 75-186

Dear Senator Carpenter:

     Thank you for your letter of September 30, 1975,
requesting an opinion with regard to the income
reporting requirements of the Political Reform Act.
Since your letter raises no substantial question
involving an interpretation of the Act, no formal
opinion will be issued. However, I hope the following
informal comments will be helpful.

     Your first question asked if a guest pass to a
high school, college, or university athletic event
must be reported under Chapter 7 of the Act, even if
the pass were never used. Since members of the public
must pay to attend school athletic events, a guest
pass obviously has value. If you receive an item of
value and do not pay equal or greater consideration
for that item, you have accepted a gift. Cal. Gov't.
Code Section 82028. Moreover, it is the acceptance
of the gift, and not the ultimate use to which it is
put, that imposes the reporting obligation under
Chapter 7. Accordingly, a guest pass would have to be
reported. The value of the guest pass is the price
of an equivalent season pass which could be purchased
by a member of the public.

     A separate answer to your second question is
unnecessary in light of the foregoing. Turning to
your third question, you asked if guest memberships
in social or fraternal organizations are reportable
under Chapter 7 of the Act. Again, the membership
would constitute a gift, and the acceptance of such a

Citation: Dennis G. Carpenter
Page 2

gift would impose a reporting obligation. The value
of the membership would have to pay in order to join the organization.

Of course, you can avoid reporting the member-
ships or the athletic passes by returning them or donat-
ing them to a charitable organization without claiming
a tax deduction within thirty days after receipt. If
you return them, you have not accepted a gift.

Finally, with regard to your fourth question,
your obligations under Chapter 7 of the Act are not
affected by any subsequent use you make of a gift,
including allowing someone else to use your gift. Once
you accept a gift, you must report it regardless of
whether you pass it on to another person.

If you have any further questions, please contact
Ted Prin, a staff attorney.

Sincerely,

[signature]
Michael Bennett
Executive Director,
for the Commission

MB:gg

DENNIS E. CARPENTER
ORANGE AND URBAN COUNTIES

CALIFORNIA LEGISLATURE

Senate
September 30, 1975

Mr. Daniel H. Lowenstein, Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
925 J Street, Suite 522
Sacramento, California 95814

Mr. Lowenstein,

I would appreciate an opinion by your Commission on four
questions which bear upon the reporting requirements
outlined in the Political Reform Act of 1974:

1. Are guest passes to high school, college or
university athletic contests sent to my
office reportable if never used? If so, what
value should be placed on them?

2. Are guest passes to high school, college or
university athletic contests sent to my office
reportable when they are used? If so, what
value should be placed on them?

3. Are guest memberships and social or fraternal
organizations sent to my office reportable if
never used? If so, what value should be placed
on them?

4. Would any of the three above items be reportable
if used by members of my staff or their families
even though members of my family and I never used
them?

I can understand the myriad of questions which must be coming
your way and realize the constraints placed upon your office
by staff limitations. I shall, however, look forward to a
response at your earliest convenience.

[signature]

DENNIS E. CARPENTER
Senator, 36th District

DEC:csp

MEMO TO FILE                                October 24, 1975


RE:   Telephone inquiry re Section 18202

FROM: Natalie


FROM: Ron Gastelum
       Metropolitan Water District
       Los Angeles


       Quasi-legislative proceeding under Section 82002
(administrative action) and 2 Cal. Admin. Code
Section 18202.

       Metropolitan Water District negotiates with
the California Department of Water Resources
concerning allocating expenditures on the state
water contract.

       The amount of water that the District will
purchase from the state is established under a
pre-existing contract. However, each year the
District incurs certain expenses in planning,
engineering, etc., which they are allowed to deduct
from the purchase price paid to DWR. Negotiations
are carried on between the District and DWR to
determine how much of a deduction will be allowed.

       Question: Are these negotiations a "quasi-
legislative proceeding" and thus subject to Prop. 9?

       Answer: In my opinion these negotiations are
carried on to determine the rights of the respective
parties under a pre-existing contract, and would not
be quasi-legislative. However, our regulation does
not specifically address this kind of question....
It may come within (a)(1) or (a)(4) of 18202.

       I told him that he can request an opinion if he
wants a formal answer - or that I will send him an
informal written answer if he sends a letter setting
out more specific facts.

sections.question:
Are these negotiations a "quasi-legislative proceeding" and thus subject to Prop. 9?

Answer: In my opinion these negotiations are carried on to determine the rights of the respective parties under a pre-existing contract, and would not be quasi-legislative. However, our regulation does not specifically address this kind of question.... It may come within (a)(1) or (a)(4) of 18202.

I told him that he can request an opinion if he wants a formal answer - or that I will send him an informal written answer if he sends a letter setting out more specific facts.
